text.title: trunk-based-dev-patterns Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for trunk-based-dev-patterns. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Branch lifetime cap is under 24 hours. Rebase onto main before opening the PR; squash on merge so trunk history stays linear.
rule.rationale: Long-lived branches accumulate conflicts and diverge from the trunk, defeating the purpose of TBD. Squash merge keeps trunk history linear and searchable.

rule.statement: One flag = one task = one cleanup ticket. File the cleanup ticket the moment the flag is born; otherwise flags accumulate to dozens and rot.
rule.rationale: Flags with no removal deadline become permanent technical debt. Cleaning up immediately after rollout prevents flag sprawl.

rule.statement: Flag naming follows <area>.<verb>_<thing> (e.g., billing.use_stripe_v2); ban boolean negatives (disable_x) because they invert mid-rollout and confuse agents.
rule.rationale: Positive naming is unambiguous. A flag named "billing.disable_legacy" inverts logic when renamed to "billing.use_new" during rollout, causing off-by-one errors in agent-generated code.

rule.statement: CI must be under 10 minutes wall time. If slower, parallelize tests, cache dependencies, and move slow integration suites to nightly jobs. Slow CI kills TBD adoption.
rule.rationale: Developers and agents disable hooks or skip CI on slow feedback loops. Sub-10-minute CI sustains the daily-merge cadence.

rule.statement: Auto-revert on red trunk: a PR that broke main is reverted by bot (git revert -m 1), not by humans. Fix forward in a fresh PR.
rule.rationale: Manual reverts are slow and error-prone. Bot-driven reverts keep trunk green and force authors to fix in a follow-up, maintaining the discipline.

rule.statement: Forbid direct push to main from anyone except automation; require linear history and signed commits.
rule.rationale: Direct pushes bypass CI and introduce merge commits. Requiring PRs ensures every change passes gates and is reviewable.

rule.statement: Run dark launches at less than 1% traffic for at least one diurnal cycle before scaling. Bug surfaces look identical at 0.01% and 1% but error budgets diverge.
rule.rationale: Low-traffic canary windows catch bugs safely. Scaling to 1% too quickly masks bugs that only appear at scale.

rule.statement: Trunk is Always Broken: Fix by gating merges on passing tests, adding pre-commit checks, speeding up CI to under 10 minutes, using feature flags for incomplete work, and keeping commits small.
rule.rationale: Fast feedback + safety gates + smaller changes = trust in trunk. Without these, developers work around TBD.

rule.statement: Need Long-Lived Branches: Fix by breaking features into smaller parts, implementing feature flags, using Branch by Abstraction, and reserving long-lived branches only for release branches or compliance gating.
rule.rationale: Long-lived branches are an anti-pattern in TBD. Chunking and flags eliminate the need for most branch lifetimes over 24 hours.

rule.statement: Code Review Bottleneck: Fix by keeping PRs under 200 lines, pairing instead of async review, enforcing a fast SLA (under 4 hours), and rotating reviewers to spread load.
rule.rationale: Small PRs are faster to review and merge. Async review is slow; live review (pair or sync check-in) unblocks faster.

rule.statement: TBD + SDD Workflow: create task in .aidocs/backlog, move to .aidocs/todo, create short-lived branch (less than 1 day), implement with feature flags, open PR, fast review, merge to trunk, move task to .aidocs/done.
rule.rationale: Each SDD task = one trunk-merged increment. Feature flags control visibility; cleanup tasks surface flag removal.

rule.statement: Feature Flags for SDD: flag name matches task ID when possible, enable after thorough testing, remove after stability confirmed.
rule.rationale: Traceability: grep the codebase and find which task introduced each flag.

rule.statement: Continuous Delivery from Trunk: trunk push triggers CI, CI triggers staging deploy. Manual or approval-gated production deploy. Feature flags control visibility, not deployment. Task done = merged to trunk. Task deployed = feature flag enabled. Task complete = flag removed.
rule.rationale: Deployment and release are decoupled. Code deploys to production frequently; flags determine what end-users see.

rule.statement: LLMs forget to remove the abstraction layer after rollout; always file the "collapse abstraction" follow-up before merging the introduction.
rule.rationale: Without explicit cleanup, the temporary abstraction becomes permanent indirection.

rule.statement: Agent generates flag checks with hard-coded True defaults during local dev and forgets to revert — gate via env (FLAGS_DEFAULT=off in CI) and grep for literal is_enabled(... True).
rule.rationale: Hard-coded True makes the flag always-on in CI and production, defeating the purpose.

rule.statement: Dark-launch comparison code re-raises in production paths — wrap in try/except at all costs; the README example does, the agent often skips it.
rule.rationale: The dark path should never crash the current path.

rule.statement: Squash-merge loses commit-level granularity; agents writing 200-line PRs as one commit are fine, agents writing 2000-line PRs need a stacked-PR workflow instead.
rule.rationale: Squash commit enables size enforcement.

rule.statement: Agent will "fix" pre-commit failures by adding # type: ignore / # noqa instead of fixing the issue; require diff review before any new pragma.
rule.rationale: Pragmas suppress the tool; they do not fix the underlying issue.

rule.statement: Human-in-loop checkpoint: any flag flip from less than 50% to 100% on a billing/auth-critical path. Agent can stage the rollout but a human approves the final percent.
rule.rationale: Critical systems warrant extra caution.

rule.statement: Branch protection state drift: agent adding a job to CI may leave main not requiring it — verify via gh api repos/:owner/:repo/branches/main/protection after CI changes.
rule.rationale: Without branch protection verification, new CI checks become advisory, not mandatory.
